title: The Effect of Computer Monitoring on Employees’ Productivity in Telecommuting Arrangements
abstract: When employees work from home, organizations have traditionally placed a stronger reliance on output-based controls because of the inability to physically observe its employees. Because of recent advances in technology, however, many organizations now use input-based controls. One such input-based control is computer monitoring, which records the number of hours spent working and leaves out any time in which an employee does not perform work activities. On the one hand, computer monitoring may provide productivity-enhancing benefits by reducing the likelihood of employees experiencing self-control problems, such as procrastination. On the other hand, computer monitoring may motivate reduced effort by decreasing employees’ autonomy, which a prior study finds is an important mediator of the relation between telecommuting and productivity. We take advantage of a field-research setting in which the organization adopts computer monitoring for its telecommuting employees. We find that computer monitoring leads to a 7.1% productivity increase for telecommuting employees. We also find that computer monitoring reduces employees’ procrastination tendencies. In addition, we find that low performers’ productivity increases more following the introduction of computer monitoring. Finally, we find evidence that telecommuters’ departure rates increase significantly following computer monitoring. Our results suggest that technology can play a significant role in expanding organizations’ management control choices when achieving controls in telecommuting arrangements.

author.Yea Hee Ko: Leadership and Organizational Science Area, Binghamton University, The State University of New York, Binghamton, New York 13902;
author.In Gyun Baek: Department of Accounting, National University of Singapore, Singapore 119245

article.1. Introduction: As the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic led to a massive experiment in telecommuting programs, organizations began to wonder how they could ensure that their employees exert their costly effort into work. Scholars mainly emphasize the importance of output-based controls, rather than input-based controls, in ensuring that employees expend their costly effort in telecommuting arrangements (Olson 1982,Kurland and Egan 1999,Gajendran and Harrison 2007).1Because of recent advances in technology that enable employers to electronically monitor their employees, however, organizations have begun to append computer monitoring, an input-based control that is designed to monitor employees’ actions and increase time spent on the job. For example, many organizations require employees to use time-tracking software that records the number of hours spent working and leaves out any time in which an employee is not engaged in work activities (Slatis 2020). However, there is little empirical evidence that employees’ productivity changes when an organization adds computer monitoring to its output-based controls in telecommuting arrangements. This study investigates whether an addition of an input-based control, computer monitoring, to output-based controls in place increases telecommuters’ productivity. It is unclear whether the addition of computer monitoring to output-based controls affects telecommuters’ productivity. On the one hand, computer monitoring can enhance performance by reducing the likelihood of employees engaging in opportunistic behavior, such as shirking, and mitigating self-control problems that can arise from distractions. On the other hand, employees may experience a decrease in autonomy and a reduced sense of responsibility following computer monitoring, which can reduce performance. We examine the productivity effects of computer monitoring in telecommuting arrangements using the work of patent examiners at the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO). Although the USPTO has allowed its patent examiners to telecommute since 2006, it faced the control problem of systemic misreporting of time and attendance (T&A), which led to a series of nationwide investigations (U.S. Department of Commerce 2014).2In response to these investigations, the USPTO began requiring its teleworkers to remotely log in to the USPTO network during their working hours on February 22, 2015. To remain logged in to the network, employees need to constantly perform work activities, which are automatically captured by mouse or keyboard movements. After 15 minutes of nonuse, the USPTO network autolocks employees’ computer screen. The network does not count the time in which employees’ computer screen is locked as working hours, which need to exceed at least 80 hours every two weeks.3Such requirements impose significant restrictions on examiners’ flexibility because they were allowed to remain offline during working hours prior to February 22, 2015.4 The USPTO provides several advantages as a setting to examine our research question. First, there was a group of telecommuting and nontelecommuting examiners when the USPTO introduced computer monitoring. Because only telecommuting examiners were subject to computer monitoring, this allows us to identify treatment (examiners who were telecommuting as of February 22, 2015) and control groups (examiners who were not telecommuting as of February 22, 2015) and employ a difference-in-differences (DiD) methodology.5 Second, we use a work-from-home (WFH) setting that occurred before the COVID-19 pandemic. This feature allows us to account for a host of confounds induced by the pandemic that might affect employees’ productivity, such as anxiety about the disease, stress from lack of social outlets, and having children at home because of school closures.6This enables us to more credibly attribute the effects on employee productivity we observe to telecommuting. Our results using propensity score matched difference-in-differences analyses indicate that the introduction of computer monitoring at the USPTO increases telecommuting examiners’ productivity.7This result is consistent with computer monitoring decreasing information asymmetry between the employers and employees and mitigating telecommuters’ self-control problems (Baiman 1990,Frakes and Wasserman 2020). Specifically, the results translate to a 7.1% increase in the total amount of work completed each month. Using two different quality metrics, we find that computer monitoring is positively associated with work quality. We also explore plausible mechanisms driving our results. First, we find that computer monitoring is associated with higher productivity on days on which employees are more likely to procrastinate on work (nondeadline days) but had no impact on productivity on days on which employees are already putting in a significant amount of effort (deadline days). In addition, our findings indicate that treatment examiners not only submit their final work outcomes earlier but also initiate their work earlier after the introduction of computer monitoring. By contrast, control examiners do not show such a pattern. These results suggest that computer monitoring reduces employees’ procrastinating tendencies. Finally, we find that computer monitoring is associated with a greater productivity increase for low performers than for high performers. Although we include individual fixed effects in our estimations to control for time-invariant differences between telecommuting and nontelecommuting examiners, we also address a concern that our findings can be attributed to telecommuting examiners experiencing contemporaneous shocks that coincided with the USPTO’s adoption of computer monitoring. For example, telecommuting employees might have experienced the USPTO’s other monitoring features that are likely unobservable, such as stricter monitoring from their supervisors and/or peers. To mitigate this concern, we assess the sensitivity of the results to unobservable heterogeneity (Oster 2019) and find that our results are not likely driven by unobservable monitoring attributes of the USPTO. Finally, we provide preliminary evidence of potential adverse effects of computer monitoring in the form of a greater increase in departure rates of telecommuters relative to nontelecommuters after computer monitoring. Further, a nontrivial number of telecommuters return to the office following computer monitoring, indicating potential dissatisfaction with monitoring. Although our results indicate that computer monitoring benefits telecommuters’ productivity, we should note that several distinctive factors at the USPTO contribute to the successful implementation of telecommuting and computer monitoring. First, the job of the USPTO’s patent examiners is independent and does not require much communication with others to complete a task. Because the USPTO’s independent work environment is far from universal, we are not able to conclude to what extent our findings for the USPTO can generalize to other settings with high levels of task interdependence.8Second, the repetitive and routine nature of the job of examiners may favor the success of computer monitoring. Our results may not hold in a work environment that shows a weaker or insignificant correlation between time spent working and output productivity, such as creative organizations (Grabner 2014,Grabner and Speckbacher 2016). These distinctive features of the USPTO suggest that the practice of computer monitoring in telecommuting arrangements is worth further exploration. Our research broadens our understanding of how organizations achieve organizationally determined goals in work-from-home settings. Although prior literature documents the overall effects of telecommuting on employee productivity (Dutcher 2012,Bloom et al. 2015), the literature is largely silent on organizations’ management control choices that affect employee productivity in telecommuting arrangements. Motivated by recent advances in technology, we examine the addition of input-based controls to existing output-based controls and document the positive effects of computer monitoring on telecommuters’ productivity. Our results suggest that technology can play a significant role in expanding organizations’ management control choices. Our study also contributes to the literature on computer monitoring by documenting that computer monitoring increases telecommuters’ productivity. Prior literature on computer monitoring provides inconclusive evidence on the effects of computer monitoring. One stream of literature contends that computer monitoring leads to improved performance and perceived fairness without the negative effects on employees’ quality of life (Griffith 1993,Stanton 2000,Hovorka-Mead et al. 2002). Another stream of literature focuses more on negative effects of computer monitoring, suggesting that computer monitoring leads to greater employee stress, anxiety, fatigue, and chronic health disorders and decreased job satisfaction (Irving et al. 1986,Grant and Higgins 1989,Smith et al. 1992,Aiello and Svec 1993). Prior papers in these literature, however, do not examine the effects on telecommuters’ productivity. Our results provide insights into whether and how computer monitoring increases telecommuters’ productivity.
article.2. Setting: To examine our research question, we take advantage of the USPTO, a federal agency that decides whether to grant patents and register trademarks. The USPTO employs approximately 9,000 patent examiners who evaluate the patentability of incoming patent applications. We focus on the work of patent examiners to examine the effect of computer monitoring on productivity in telecommuting arrangements. 2.1. Patent Examination Process Patent examination at the USPTO is characterized by structured workflow and procedures (Cockburn et al. 2002). When an inventor submits a patent application—a document specifying an invention and a set of an inventor’s claims around the invention—to the USPTO, it is first routed to an “Art Unit,” an operational team of examiners at the USPTO that consists of 10–30 examiners responsible for examining similar technologies. Upon arrival, a supervisory patent examiner (SPE) in the Art Unit assigns an application to a specific examiner in a quasirandom fashion (Lemley and Sampat 2012,Sampat and Williams 2019,Farre-Mensa et al. 2020,Hegde et al. 2022). Therefore, an examiner has no control over which patent applications to review. In most cases, an examiner assigned to an application has continuing responsibility during the entire examination process of the application. An examiner begins the examination by reading the application and extensively searching for previous patents, patent applications, or other publications (“prior art”) to determine the patentability of the claimed invention. After completing a prior art search, an examiner composes an “office action” that delineates the decision to grant or reject patents on the claimed invention and provides reasons for such a decision. The examination typically ends when an examiner grants a patent on an application or when an inventor abandons an application by not responding to an examiner’s rejection within six months. 2.2. Telecommuting Arrangements and Computer Monitoring To save costs for office space and enhance work-life balance for employees, the USPTO started its telecommuting program for examiners in 2006. To be eligible for telecommuting, examiners must have worked at the USPTO for at least two years and achieved civil servant grade level (General Schedule (GS) level) GS-12.9At first, the public and business press praised the USPTO’s telecommuting program because it minimizes office space, reduces greenhouse gas emissions, enhances the USPTO’s resiliency during weather-related closures, and helps retain highly skilled workforce (Hochmuth 2014). However, several whistleblower complaints alleging systemic T&A abuse by telecommuting examiners prompted the USPTO to conduct an internal investigation. Key allegations contained in complaints were examiners misrepresenting their T&A and only working at the end of the quota periods. The investigation soon led to nationwide scrutiny into telecommuters’ T&A abuse at the USPTO by members of Congress, federal agencies, and the business press.10Examples of T&A abuse by telecommuters at the USPTO include playing golf and socializing while on the clock (Hochmuth 2014,Clark 2015). In response to the investigations into telecommuters’ T&A abuse and the deficiency of effective internal control systems, the USPTO began requiring its full-time teleworkers to remain logged in to the USPTO network during their working hours on February 22, 2015 (“Teleworker Policy”).11Prior to the Teleworker Policy, teleworkers were able to “telework without turning on his or her computer to access the USPTO network” (U.S. Department of Commerce 2016, p. 17). After the Telework Policy, however, all teleworkers are required to remotely log in to the USPTO network using a virtual private network or small office home office router during their working hours. The USPTO network captures the time and type of user activities, including log ons, log offs, and all records in between. To remain logged in to the USPTO network, examiners need to constantly perform work activities. The USPTO network automatically spots examiners’ work activities by the movement of a mouse or keyboard strokes. If examiners do not move their mouse or keyboard for 15 minutes, the USPTO network autolocks their computer screen, and examiners are no longer connected to the network. The network does not count the time in which examiners are not connected to the network as working hours. If the network detects two consecutive work activities from an examiner that are apart for less than 15 minutes, it assumes the examiner is performing work activities continuously. Because of this feature of the network, examiners can still game the system by just remembering to move the mouse or tapping keys on the keyboard, adding tension to our research question of whether computer monitoring indeed has a productivity-enhancing benefit. The Teleworker Policy applies to all full-time teleworkers unless they experience power outages or are engaged in official duties, such as attending training sessions, attending meetings, or traveling for official business. The Teleworker Policy also mandates that telecommuters do not block available communication methods, such as setting a “do not disturb” status on the internal communication tools (U.S. Department of Commerce 2016).12Because (1) the USPTO does not count the time an employee is not connected to the network as working hours and (2) examiners must reach or exceed the requirement of 80 working hours every two weeks (U.S. Department of Commerce 2016),13the Teleworker Policy imposes significant restrictions on examiners. Examiners’ failure to comply with T&A obligations may result in disciplinary actions, which could include termination or the requirement to repay the USPTO any improperly received pay. 2.3. Control Systems at the USPTO The USPTO adopts several control systems to ensure that telecommuters exert their costly effort into work. First, the USPTO sets the number of applications that each examiner needs to review in a given period of time. An examiner is given more time to review an application if the underlying technology of an application is more complex and the examiner’s GS level is lower. For example, a GS-12 examiner needs to review an application in the “fishing lures” technology field in 16.6 hours, and a same-rank examiner needs to review an application in the “satellite communication” technology field in 27.7 hours. In addition, compared with a GS-12 examiner, a GS-7 (GS-14) examiner is given 30% more time (35% less time) to review an application in the same technology field. The number of hours allotted to examiners determines the workload goal (“quota”) that an examiner is expected to achieve biweekly and quarterly.14 In addition to meeting the quota by the deadline, the USPTO evaluates examiners’ performance based on whether they complete office actions in a timely manner (“docket management”). The USPTO’s emphasis on docket management is consistent with the USPTO’s mission, which is to ensure a “timely examination of patent applications” (U.S. Patent and Trademark Office 2021, p. 65). Further, the USPTO considers examiners’ work quality (i.e., examination quality) in evaluating their performance. Every quarter, the Art Unit’s SPEs evaluate at least one office action of each examiner in their Art Units. Specifically, SPEs check for clear errors to determine whether examiners in their Art Units correctly approve or reject applications on which they make office actions. However, the examination quality element of examiners’ performance evaluation plans is incomplete. This is partly because SPEs’ ratings on the examination quality element should start from the highest rating, and SPEs can reduce the ratings only when they can prove that examiners’ performance is lower than the highest rating. This feature leads to concerns that SPEs do not adequately discriminate among examiners in their Art Units in terms of their performance ratings.15An internal report by the USPTO also indicates that the examination quality element is “too difficult and complex to apply and enforce” to guard against telecommuters’ T&A abuse (U.S. Patent and Trademark Office 2013, p. 12). Finally, the USPTO requires telecommuters to “check and respond appropriately to voicemail and e-mail periodically throughout the workday during … business hours” (U.S. Patent and Trademark Office 2013, p. 6). The USPTO enforces this policy because electronic communication, such as voicemail and email, is the primary means for SPEs to interact with examiners in their Art Units. However, this “communication requirement” is not an effective check on telecommuters’ T&A abuse because the USPTO does “not define how often ‘periodically’ means or when is ‘appropriate’ for [telecommuters] to respond” to voicemail and email (U.S. Patent and Trademark Office 2013, p. 6). Importantly, the controls described, except for the communication requirement, are largely the same for both telecommuters and nontelecommuters except that “physical presence can serve as an additional control” for nontelecommuters (U.S. Patent and Trademark Office 2013, p. 4). In addition, these controls did not change following implementation of the Teleworker Policy on February 22, 2015. In sum, prior to the Teleworker Policy, whereas nontelecommuters were subject to both output-based controls (quota, docket management, and examination quality) and input-based control (physical observation by supervisors and peers), telecommuters were subject to the same level of output-based controls and a weak and ineffective form of input-based control (communication requirement). Using a group of nontelecommuters who are subject to both output- and input-based controls as our benchmark, we examine whether theadditionof an input-based control, computer monitoring, to output-based controls in place increases telecommuters’ productivity.
article.3. Data, Research Design, and Descriptiv: 3.1. Data and Sample We construct our data set combining multiple sources to examine the productivity implications of computer monitoring at the USPTO. First, via the filing of Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests, we obtain data on the year in which each examiner joined the USPTO and each examiner’s grade level (GS level) each year.16In addition, the USPTO also provides information on whether each examiner participated in the telecommuting program and the exact date, month, and year in which each examiner began telecommuting. This information allows us to identify whether each examiner was telecommuting when the USPTO introduced computer monitoring on February 22, 2015. Second, we use data from the Public Patent Application Information Retrieval (Public PAIR) that contains daily information on a number of characteristics of each patent application. From the Public PAIR, we collect information on all transactions implemented by each examiner to measure an examiner’s aggregate output. Specifically, we followChoudhury et al. (2021)and measure examiner productivity using the number of office actions that each examiner completes in each month (# Office Actions), which is the basis of the USPTO’s internal performance evaluation for examiners. An office action is a patent decision by an examiner that indicates whether a patent application is allowed or rejected. Table 1summarizes the sample selection process. We begin with the data on patent examiners who worked at the USPTO from 2001 to 2017 that we obtain through an FOIA request. Next, we exclude examiners for whom we are unable to calculate productivity measures. We limit our analyses to examiners who joined the USPTO after 2007 as the Public PAIR did not provide data on prior art citations by examiners until late 2007.17We also limit our analyses to examiner-month observations using three criteria. (1) We have the data on examiners’ gender and ethnicity, (2) they fall within our study period, and (3) we are able to match examiners with their Art Unit-level measures. Finally, we perform a one-on-one matching to identify our treatment and control examiners, resulting in a final sample of 840 examiners who correspond to 9,143 examiner-month observations.18 Table 1.Sample Selection Table 1.Sample Selection Note. This table summarizes the sample selection process. 3.2. Research Design We examine our research question using the following examiner-month-level Ordinary Lease Squares regression with a DiD methodology: Our set of analyses examines the 12-month window (6 months before and after) surrounding the introduction of computer monitoring at the USPTO that occurred on February 22, 2015.19We use the relatively short 12-month window because DiD estimates are more reliable when using a short time window, and the use of long prepost periods increases the likelihood of capturing confounding events (Duflo 2002).20The dependent variable is log(# Office Actions), defined as the natural logarithm of# Office Actions.Treatmentis an indicator variable equal to one for treatment examiners and zero otherwise.Postis an indicator variable equal to one for the six-month window subsequent to February of 2015 and zero in the six-month window prior to February of 2015. Our variable of interest is the interaction termTreatment×Post, which measures the relative change in productivity of treatment examiners following the introduction of computer monitoring relative to control examiners. We define all variables inAppendix A. Because only telecommuting examiners were subject to computer monitoring, we define examiners who were telecommuting when the USPTO began utilizing computer monitoring (February 22, 2015) as treatment examiners. The USPTO operates two prominent telecommuting programs: the WFH program and the work-from-anywhere (WFA) program (Choudhury et al. 2021). Examiners participating in the WFH program work from home four days a week and work in the office once a week. Those participating in the WFA program can live anywhere in the United States. We use both WFH and WFA examiners as treatment examiners.21 We use propensity score matching to identify control examiners. Specifically, for each treatment examiner, we find a control examiner with a similar propensity to telecommute as of February 22, 2015 but did not do so. We match the control examiners based on the following characteristics: each examiner’s gender and ethnicity, the total number of office actions completed by each examiner that is averaged over all preperiod months, and the number of overtime and total examining hours per examiner of an examiner’s team in a given year that is averaged over all preperiod months.22In addition, we require all control examiners to have joined the USPTO in the same year and month with their matched treatment examiners. This requirement ensures that all treatment-control pairs have the exact same tenure (measured in months), reducing biases that could emerge from unobservable task-specific human capital of examiners that varies with tenure (Frakes and Wasserman 2017,Choudhury et al. 2021). Finally, we require all treatment (control) examiners to telecommute (not telecommute) in all of the 12-month estimation window. For the control examiners, we use the six-month window subsequent to February of 2015 as quasipostevent months and the six-month window prior to February of 2015 as quasipre-event months. For these examiners, we once again include the 12-month window surrounding February of 2015 and exclude the pseudoevent month (February of 2015) from our sample. We include individual fixed effects to control for time-invariant individual characteristics and year-month fixed effects to account for variation in examiner productivity over time. We also include GS-level fixed effects to control for variation in examiner productivity by examination time constraints that differ based on GS levels (Lemley and Sampat 2012,Frakes and Wasserman 2017). We include tenure fixed effects to account for variation in productivity by experience levels (Choudhury et al. 2021). Finally, we cluster standard errors at the individual examiner level to correct for autocorrelation within given examiners over time. We note two potential issues with our research design. First, although an ideal control group would consist of telecommuters not experiencing computer monitoring, we use the group of nontelecommuters not receiving computer monitoring as a benchmark because of the absence of an ideal control group. Therefore, our research represents “the nonequivalent comparison group design,” which “may be the most common of all quasi-experiments” (Shadish et al. 2002, p. 136).Shadish et al. (2002, p. 159) suggest that a researcher can strengthen the inferences of the nonequivalent comparison group design by using “a nonequivalent comparison group deliberately chosen to have maximum pretest similarity to the treatment group on as many observed characteristics as possible.” By matching on many observed characteristics of examiners, we attempt to strengthen the counterfactual inference. Further,Shadish et al. (2002, p. 159) also suggest that cohort controls (e.g., of the same age, same general socioeconomic status, etc.) are a particularly useful comparison. Following this suggestion, we require all control examiners to have the exact same tenure (measured in months) with their matched treatment examiners (i.e., cohort controls). Second, the treatment and control groups can communicate with each other at our research site. Therefore, we cannot rule out the possibility that treatment examiners might have positively responded to cues about expected behavior (i.e., productivity increases) if they realized nontelecommuters did not experience computer monitoring. 3.3. Descriptive Statistics Panel A ofTable 2presents descriptive statistics for variables in our analyses. The average number of office actions for each examiner in each month,# Office Actions, is 11.792. Our final sample includes a total of 840 examiners. Because we use one-to-one matching, we have the same number of treatment and control examiners (420 examiners each). Among 420 treatment examiners, 168 examiners participated in the WFA program, and 252 examiners participated in the WFH program. An average examiner has job tenure of 5.312 years at the USPTO as of 2015. On average, a treatment examiner has worked from home for 24.645 months as of February of 2015; 25.6% of examiners are female. Table 2.Descriptive Statistics Table 2.Descriptive Statistics Notes. Panel A reports the descriptive statistics on the variables in our analyses and the sample composition. Panel B presents the average values of the variables used in our analyses for treatment and control examiners in the pre- and postcomputer monitoring periods, respectively. Q, Quartile. **Two-tailed statistical significance at the 5% level; ***two-tailed statistical significance at the 1% level. We also display the univariate changes in the variables used in our analyses in panel B ofTable 2. In the preadoption period, the difference in the average value of# Office Actionsbetween treatment and control examiners is not statistically significant (11.460 versus 11.428; two-tailedp> 0.10). However, in the postadoption period, the average value of# Office Actionsfor treatment examiners is greater than that for control examiners (12.529 versus 11.740; two-tailedp< 0.01). The difference in differences is also positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.01), providing preliminary evidence that treatment examiners’ productivity increases following computer monitoring.23
article.4. Empirical Results: 4.1. Main Test We present the results of estimating Model (1) inTable 3. The regression results reported in columns (1)–(3) inTable 3indicate that computer monitoring increases employee productivity. The difference across these models is the inclusion of differentFixed Effects. In all models, the estimated coefficient on the interaction term,Treatment×Post, is positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.01 for all models). For example, the coefficient estimate of 0.069 in column (3) suggests that the introduction of computer monitoring led to a 7.1% (=(e0.069− 1) × 100) increase in# Office Actionsfor telecommuters relative to nontelecommuters. This finding is consistent with computer monitoring providing productivity-enhancing benefits for telecommuters.24 Table 3.Effects on Telecommuters’ Productivity Table 3.Effects on Telecommuters’ Productivity Notes. This table presents the results of estimating Model (1). Allt-statistics (in parentheses) are based on standard errors clustered at the individual level.# Office Actionsis the number of office actions that each examiner completes in each month.Treatmentis an indicator variable equal to one for treatment examiners and zero otherwise.Postis an indicator variable equal to one for the six-month window subsequent to February of 2015 and zero in the six-month window prior to February of 2015. *Two-tailed statistical significance at the 10% level; ***two-tailed statistical significance at the 1% level. In the online appendix, we assess the parallel trends assumption and find little evidence of differential time trends between the treatment and control examiners prior to the introduction of computer monitoring. We also assess another necessary assumption for a DiD methodology, the SUTVA, and conclude that the SUTVA is not likely violated in our setting. 4.2. Effects on Work Quality The results discussed are consistent with an increase in productivity following the introduction of computer monitoring. However, computer monitoring may lead to a decrease in quality of work if treatment examiners compromise quality of work for the increase in productivity. To explore this possibility, we follow prior literature and construct two proxies for work quality. First, we use the monthly proportion of citations added by examiners that are used for the first time in reviewing patent applications (% New Cites) as an input measure that is informative about examiners’ search efforts. Prior research suggests that% New Citesis a useful metric for assessing examiners’ efforts in finding relevant prior art, a crucial step in accurately assessing the patentability of an invention. For example,Baek (2023)suggests that examiners are expected to conduct thorough searches of prior art in order to accurately assess the patentability of an invention. Second, we use the incidence of requests for continued examination (RCEs) to capture examiners’ work quality. When dissatisfied with the review process, applicants file RCEs to keep pursuing their applications upon rejections (Frakes and Wasserman 2019,2020;Baek 2023). Therefore, a high degree of RCEs represents low-quality reviews. Specifically, we use% RCEas a proxy for work quality, defined as the monthly percentage of first office actions filed by an examiner that resulted in RCEs. As shown in panel A ofTable 2, the average value of% New Citesis 51.2%, meaning that 51.2% of examiner-added citations are first used by a given examiner each month. The average value of% RCEis 45.6%, indicating that a total of 45.6% of first office actions ultimately led to RCEs. We replace the dependent variable in Model (1), log(# Office Actions), with one of the two dependent variables described earlier and re-estimate Model (1). The results presented inTable 4suggest that the introduction of computer monitoring positively affects work quality. Panels A and B present the regression results when the dependent variables are% New Citesand% RCE, respectively. The difference across columns (1)–(3) in both panels is the inclusion of different fixed effects. In panel A, the positive and statistically significant coefficients on the interaction term,Treatment×Post, suggest that computer monitoring is associated with telecommuters using more prior art that they did not use before. In panel B, the negative and statistically significant coefficients on the interaction term,Treatment×Post, suggest that computer monitoring leads to a decrease in the likelihood of applicants filing RCEs in response to office actions issued by telecommuters. Taken together, these results suggest that computer monitoring is positively associated with two dimensions of work quality. Table 4.Effects on Work Quality Table 4.Effects on Work Quality Notes. This table presents the results of estimating Model (1) after replacing log(# Office Actions) with% New Citesand% RCE.% New Citesis the monthly proportion of citations added by examiners that are used for the first time in reviewing patent applications.% RCEis the monthly percentage of first office actions filed by an examiner that resulted in RCEs. Allt-statistics (in parentheses) are based on standard errors clustered at the individual level. *Two-tailed statistical significance at the 10% level; **two-tailed statistical significance at the 5% level; ***two-tailed statistical significance at the 1% level. 4.3. Mechanism Test 4.3.1. Examiner-Month-Level Analyses. Although the results inTables 3and4showwhethercomputer monitoring has productivity-enhancing benefits, they do not shed light onhowcomputer monitoring increases telecommuters’ productivity. To explore the channels through which computer monitoring influences productivity, we conduct a test as discussed. We build on theories that computer monitoring can reduce telecommuters’ self-control problems. One pervasive problem with examiners’ work at the USPTO is that although examiners need to hit their production targets over a biweekly or quarterly period, they complete a substantial volume of work at the end of quota periods. This “end-loading” behavior has received notable attention from commentators, policymakers, and the national media. For example, the Office of Inspector General for the Commerce Department, the USPTO’s parent agency, indicates that end loading is a common work practice at the USPTO and has the potential to lead to poor-quality patent examinations.Frakes and Wasserman (2020)find that the submission of completed reviews of applications spikes on the last day of a quota period. Further, they find evidence that examiners end load their reviews at notably higher rates immediately upon telecommuting, consistent with telecommuting exacerbating self-control problems. We argue that computer monitoring reduces employees’ procrastination tendencies. AsFrakes and Wasserman (2020, p. 4)contend, if examiners’ procrastination behavior intensifies upon telecommuting as “external monitoring and supervision [are] decreased” in telecommuting arrangements, we expect computer monitoring to reduce examiners’ propensity to procrastinate on work efforts because of its monitoring nature. To gauge the clustering of work submissions of examiners, we plot inFigure 1the number of office actions by the number of days until the last day of each biweekly period.Figure 1shows that the submission of complete reviews of applications spikes on the last day of each biweekly period. This pattern is consistent withFrakes and Wasserman (2020, p. 3), who find evidence that examiners “bunch their examination reviews at the end of quota periods.” To examine the possibility that computer monitoring enhances examiners’ productivity by reducing their procrastination problems, we decompose the number of office actions by each examiner in each month into (1) the number of office actions completed on the last day of quota periods (# Office Actions Deadline) and (2) the number of office actionsnotcompleted on the last day of quota periods (#Office Actions Non-Deadline). We focus only on the number of office actions completed at deadlines versus nondeadlines asFigure 1shows a spike in output at deadlines but not at nondeadlines. Of the 12-month window we use (368 days), we identify 29 days as the last day of the quota periods, suggesting that 7.9% (=29/368) is the last day of the quota periods in our sample (untabulated). As shown in panel A ofTable 2, of 11.792 office actions by each examiner in each month, an examiner completes 2.234 office actions on the last day of the quota periods, representing 18.9% (=2.234/11.792) of the total number of office actions by each examiner in each month. This suggests that on the last day of the quota periods, an examiner completes 2.4 times (=18.9%/7.9%) the number of office actions an examiner completes on an average day. The results presented inTable 5indicate that the increase in# Office Actionsfollowing the introduction of computer monitoring is driven by office actions completed on nondeadline days. Panels A and B present the regression results when the dependent variables are log(1 +# Office Actions Deadline) and log(1 +# Office Actions Non-Deadline), respectively. The difference across columns (1)–(3) of both panels is the inclusion of different fixed effects. The coefficient on the interaction termTreatment×Postis not statistically significant in panel A (two-tailedp> 0.10 in all models) but is positive and statistically significant in panel B (two-tailedp< 0.01 in all models). These findings suggest that the increase in examiners’ productivity following the introduction of computer monitoring is driven by the increase in productivity on nondeadline days on which examiners are more likely to procrastinate. Table 5.Mechanism Test: Examiner-Month-Level Analyses Table 5.Mechanism Test: Examiner-Month-Level Analyses Notes. Panels A and B present the results of estimating Model (1) after replacing log(# Office Actions) with log(1 +# Office Actions Deadline) and log(1 +# Office Actions Non-Deadline), respectively. Allt-statistics (in parentheses) are based on standard errors clustered at the individual level.# Office Actions Deadlineis the number of office actions completed on the last day of quota periods.# Office Actions Non-Deadlineis the number of office actionsnot completed on the last day of quota periods. ***Two-tailed statistical significance at the 1% level. 4.3.2. Application-Level Analyses. One may argue that our results presented inSection 4.3.1do not provide conclusive evidence for the effectiveness of computer monitoring in reducing examiners’ propensity to procrastinate as the submission timing of tasks may not accurately reflect the timing of actual work efforts. Therefore, to ensure that we capture the progression in actual work efforts themselves, we use the Patent Examination Data System (PEDS) that allows the public to observe examiners’ search notes that contain the information on prior art searches performed by the examiner during the examination of each patent application.25Critical for our study is information on when the examiner performed such searches (“time stamps”) using the USPTO’s internal search tools, namely the Examiner’s Automated Search Tool. As examiners typically begin the examination with prior art searches to assess the patentability of an application (Frakes and Wasserman 2017), we use search time stamps in examiners’ search notes as a proxy for the timing of underlying work efforts.Appendix Bprovides an example of an examiner’s search notes. We identify all patent applications on which treatment and control examiners complete office actions during our sample period, resulting in a sample of 80,497 applications. To economize on the costs of hand collection of data while maintaining the generalizability of our results, we randomly select 1,500 applications from this sample and download search notes of each application. For our random sample of 1,500 applications, we obtain the date on which an examiner performs prior art searches and eliminate 196 observations for which we are unable to identify the date on which an examiner performs prior art searches from the PEDS, resulting in 1,304 applications. For each application, we measure the distance (measured in days) between the date on which an examiner performs prior art searches and the last day of the quota period (Distance to Deadline).26Panel A ofTable 6shows that the average ofDistance to Deadlineis 5.687, indicating that an examiner performs prior art searches 5.687 days before the last day of a quota period on average. Table 6.Mechanism Test: Application-Level Analyses Table 6.Mechanism Test: Application-Level Analyses Notes. Panel A reports the descriptive statistics on the variableDistance to Deadline.Distance to Deadlineis the number of days between the day on which an examiner performs prior art searches and the last day of the quota period on an application. Panel B presents the average value ofDistance to Deadlinefor treatment and control examiners in the pre- and postcomputer monitoring periods, respectively. Panel C reports the results of estimating Model (2). Allt-statistics (in parentheses) are based on standard errors clustered at the individual level. Q, Quartile. **Two-tailed statistical significance at the 5% level; ***two-tailed statistical significance at the 1% level. Using the final sample of 1,304 applications, we first display the univariate changes inDistance to Deadlinein panel B ofTable 6. In the preadoption period, the average value ofDistance to Deadlinefor treatment examiners is lower than that for control examiners (5.033 versus 5.508), but the difference is statistically insignificant (two-tailedp> 0.10). However, in the postadoption period, the average value ofDistance to Deadlinefor treatment examiners is greater than that for control examiners (6.855 versus 5.262), and the difference is statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.01). This result is consistent with treatment examiners reducing the propensity to procrastinate on work efforts following the adoption of computer monitoring. The difference in differences is also positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.01). In terms of economic magnitude, the relative increase inDistance to Deadlineby treatment examiners is 2.068, representing 36% (=2.068/5.687) of the sample average. Next, we conduct an application-level multivariate analysis. Specifically, we test whether computer monitoring alleviates the propensity to procrastinate by estimating the following regression: All variables are defined. We control for the same set of fixed effects as in Model (1). We present the results of estimating Model (2) inTable 6, panel C. The regression results reported in columns (1)–(3) inTable 6, panel C indicate that computer monitoring reduces the propensity to procrastinate on work efforts. The difference across these models is the inclusion of different fixed effects. In all models, the estimated coefficient on the interaction term,Treatment×Post, is positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.05 for all models). For example, the coefficient estimate of 0.324 in column (3) suggests that the introduction of computer monitoring led to a 38.3% (=(e0.324− 1) × 100) increase inDistance to Deadlinefor telecommuters relative to nontelecommuters. Taken together, our results provide evidence that computer monitoring reduces employees’ self-control problems. 4.4. Cross-Sectional Tests Based on Prior Performance We examine whether examiners‘ prior performance moderates the effect of computer monitoring on examiners’ productivity. As discussed, to the extent that computer monitoring reduces examiners’ self-control problems, we predict that an increase in productivity will be greater for low performers than for high performers following computer monitoring. Specifically, we estimate the following regression: Low Performeris an indicator equal to one if the average of# Office Actionsprior to February of 2015 is below median and zero otherwise. All other variables are defined. We control for the same set of fixed effects as in Model (1). The results presented inTable 7suggest that among treatment examiners, low performers’ productivity increases more than high performers’ productivity following the adoption of computer monitoring. In column (2), we find a marginally significant and positive coefficient on the triple-interaction termTreatment×Post×Low Performerwhen we do not include any fixed effects (two-tailedp= 0.11). Columns (4) and (6) show that the coefficient on the triple-interaction termTreatment×Post×Low Performeris positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.10 andp< 0.05, respectively). The coefficient estimate of 0.06 in column (6) indicates that low performers increase productivity by 6.2% (=(e0.060− 1) × 100) more than high performers following computer monitoring. In sum, we find evidence that employees’ prior performance positively moderates the effect of computer monitoring on employees’ productivity.27 Table 7.Treatment Effect Heterogeneity by Prior Performance Table 7.Treatment Effect Heterogeneity by Prior Performance Notes. This table presents the results of estimating Model (3). Allt-statistics (in parentheses) are based on standard errors clustered at the individual level.Low Performeris an indicator equal to one if the average of# Office Actionsprior to February of 2015 is below median and zero otherwise. *Two-tailed statistical significance at the 10% level; **two-tailed statistical significance at the 5% level; ***two-tailed statistical significance at the 1% level.
article.5. Additional Tests: 5.1. Addressing Endogeneity In this section, we address concerns that treatment examiners experienced contemporaneous shocks that coincided with the USPTO’s adoption of computer monitoring on February 22, 2015. The USPTO introduced computer monitoring in response to the T&A abuse scandals at the USPTO and the ensuing investigations across the nation. Because these scandals and investigations reshaped the USPTO’s monitoring of telecommuting employees, multiple mechanisms could contribute to (or moderate) the main results. For example, the USPTO implemented other preventive measures around the adoption date, such as stricter monitoring by their supervisors, improved quality control procedures, and increased awareness of potential T&A abuse (National Academy of Public Administration 2015).28If so, the results we observe could reflect the effects of the USPTO’s other monitoring attributes that are likely unobservable rather than an association with computer monitoring. To explore the potential severity of this potential endogeneity effect, we use the methodology ofOster (2019)that estimates how much of a role unobservable factors must play in our setting to nullify our estimates. Specifically,Oster (2019)estimates the effect of unobservables by comparing the coefficient on the variable of interest and theR2from a “short” regression that does not include control variables (i.e., column (1) ofTable 3) with those from an “intermediate” regression that includes all measurable control variables (i.e., column (3) ofTable 3). TheOster (2019)methodology also requires an assumption about theR2of a hypothetical regression that contains both observable and unobservable variables (𝑅2𝑚𝑎𝑥Rmax2). FollowingOster (2019), we assume that𝑅2𝑚𝑎𝑥Rmax2is equal to 1.3 times theR2of the intermediate regression. We apply these conditions and obtain the Oster “delta (δ),” the degree to which unobservables must play to render a null effect. In addition, we follow the recommendations ofOster (2019)and recover the bounds for the “true”βcoefficients using two parameters, theR2andδ. We report the results of these tests inTable 8. Column (1) reportsδ= 2.535, indicating that unobservable monitoring attributes of the USPTO must be two and a half times as important as observable, controlled factors to produce no treatment effect (i.e.,β= 0). Given that column (3) ofTable 3already controls for individual, time, rank, and tenure fixed effects, it is unlikely that endogeneity stemming from the USPTO’s unobservable monitoring attributes explains our results.29In addition, column (2) reports that the trueβis likely bounded at [0.057, 0.069].Oster (2019)proposes that if (1) the likely bound for “true”βfalls within the 99.5% confidence interval for the estimatedβin column (3) ofTable 3([0.027, 0.110]) and (2) the bound excludes zero, then the estimatedβin column (3) ofTable 3is not likely driven by unobservable monitoring attributes of the USPTO. Overall, these results provide us with increased confidence that unobservable monitoring attributes of the USPTO do not explain our results. Table 8.Endogeneity Test: TheOster (2019)Method Table 8.Endogeneity Test: TheOster (2019)Method Notes. This table reports the results of tests to (1) evaluate how much of a role unobservable factors must play to driveβto zero, and (2) recover the bounds for the “true”βcoefficients. FollowingOster (2019), we assume that theR2from a hypothetical regression that controls for observable and unobservable factors is equal to 1.3 times theR2of the intermediate regression that includes all measurable control variables (i.e., column (3) ofTable 3). 5.2. Departure and Return-to-Office Behaviors After Computer Monitoring Although we provide evidence that computer monitoring leads to an increase in telecommuters’ productivity, media reports indicate that employees might experience reduced trust toward management, increased workplace stress, or reduced job satisfaction following computer monitoring (Abril and Harwell 2021). Therefore, we extend the analysis and take preliminary steps toward exploring the potential harmful effects of computer monitoring. Specifically, we investigate telecommuters’ departure behaviors following computer monitoring because prior literature in management points to employee departure as a key outcome of dysfunctions in psychological processes, such as job satisfaction and stress (Tett and Meyer 1993,Hausknecht et al. 2009,Hom et al. 2017). Although we cannot directly observe telecommuters’ psychological states (e.g., job satisfaction, workplace stress, etc.), we explore key employee behaviors that reflect such psychological states to investigate potential consequences of computer monitoring in a broader context. Panels A and B ofTable 9show descriptive evidence of whether telecommuters leave the USPTO to a greater extent following computer monitoring in the period 2012–2017. We include in this sample all examiners working at the USPTO at any point in the period 2012–2017.30We classify examiners as telecommuters in a given month if they were telecommuting at any point in that month. Panel A shows that the departure ratio of telecommuters increases significantly following computer monitoring. Specifically, panel A indicates that although 0.64%, 0.56%, and 0.66% of full-time telecommuters leave the USPTO in the preperiod of 2012, 2013, and 2014, respectively, 2.17%, 1.98%, and 1.89% of full-time telecommuters leave the USPTO in the postperiod of 2015, 2016, and 2017, respectively. In comparison, we report in panel B the departure ratio of nontelecommuters eligible for telecommuting. We identify examiners as nontelecommuters eligible for telecommuting if nontelecommuters are GS-12 or above and have tenure of two years or more. Although the ratio of nontelecommuters’ departure (2.29%) is higher than that of telecommuters (1.32%), the departure ratio of nontelecommuters does not seem to increase significantly following computer monitoring. Specifically, the ratio of nontelecommuters’ departure remains relatively constant at around 2%–3% in both pre- and postperiods. Table 9.Telecommuters’ Departure and Return-to-Office Behaviors Table 9.Telecommuters’ Departure and Return-to-Office Behaviors Notes. Panel A reports the number of telecommuters who leave the USPTO by departure month and year in the period 2012–2017, which is a six-year period surrounding the adoption of computer monitoring. Panel B reports the departure counts of nontelecommuters who were eligible for telecommuting but did not telecommute during the same period. We identify examiners as nontelecommuters eligible for telecommuting if nontelecommuters are GS-12 or above and have tenure of two years or more. Panel C presents the univariate changes in the departure ratio of telecommuters and nontelecommuters in the pre- and postadoption periods (p-values in parentheses). Panel D reports the number of telecommuters who stop telecommuting and return to the office in the period of 2015–2017. In Panels A, B, and D, the number of total employees reported in the second column from the right indicates the number of total employees at the beginning of each year. aThe month in which the USPTO adopts computer monitoring. We also display the univariate changes in the departure ratio of telecommuters and nontelecommuters in panel C ofTable 9. For telecommuters, the difference in the average value of the monthly departure ratio between the pre- and postadoption period is positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.01). However, for nontelecommuters, the difference in the average value of monthly departure ratio between the pre- and postadoption period is negative and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.05). The difference in differences is also positive and statistically significant (two-tailedp< 0.01), suggesting that telecommuters show higher departure rates following computer monitoring compared with that of nontelecommuters. Overall, these results indicate that telecommuters show higher departure rates that likely reflect reduced job satisfaction and increased workplace stress. Although we use departure rates to capture variation in the extent to which telecommuters experienced reduced job satisfaction and increased workplace stress, it is important to consider the limitation of this analysis. Specifically, this analysis does not speak to telecommuters’ outside job opportunities that are likely limited by their preferences for geographic flexibility. Jobs outside of the USPTO were less likely to provide an opportunity to work from home as extensively as the USPTO in our sample period, which is prior to the COVID-19 pandemic. Therefore, to the extent that telecommuters value an option to work from home in moving to other jobs, our results provide a lower bound for the effects of computer monitoring on telecommuters’ departure rates. As more organizations provide an option to work from home following the pandemic, the effects of computer monitoring on telecommuters’ departure rates may be stronger in today’s environments. To find additional evidence of potential harmful effects of computer monitoring, we explore telecommuters’ return-to-office behavior.31Panel D shows the number of telecommuters who return to the office from 2015 to 2017. One caveat of this data set is that we do not observe telecommuters’ return to office before 2015 as this data set is not available before 2015.32As the USPTO introduced computer monitoring in early 2015, we are not able to observe thechangesin return-to-office behaviors around the adoption date. With this caveat in mind, we find that in the period 2015–2017, a total of 207 telecommuters return to the office. The ratio of telecommuters’ return to office is 1.83% on average in the same period. Taken together, these initial descriptive findings suggest that telecommuters may potentially experience negative effects from computer monitoring that are not fully captured in their performance as reflected in their higher departure rates and nontrivial return-to-office behavior. However, we also caution that our results on telecommuters’ departure and return-to-office behaviors are descriptive and do not provide causal evidence.
article.6. Conclusion: We examine whether computer monitoring influences employees’ productivity. Specifically, we use the changes in the network log-in requirement for telecommuting examiners at the USPTO as a setting to examine our research question. Our collective evidence suggests that computer monitoring increases telecommuters’ productivity. We also find evidence that computer monitoring reduces employees’ self-control problems (i.e., procrastination). Finally, we find that low performers increase their productivity more following computer monitoring. The results of this study should be viewed in light of the following limitations. First, the USPTO provides a clean output measure of productivity (i.e., the number of office actions). Without a clean measure of productivity, computer monitoring may decrease output productivity if telecommuters prioritize time spent working that is controllable and measurable by the employer (Bonner and Sprinkle 2002,Hecht et al. 2012,Jung and Shin 2022). Second, we lack data to investigate possible psychological dysfunctions of computer monitoring. For example, employees might experience reduced trust toward management, frustrations, and dissatisfactions with work following computer monitoring. Although we examine the likely consequences of computer monitoring by investigating telecommuters’ departure and return-to-office behaviors, our data do not provide a complete picture of how employees behave in response to computer monitoring. Third, computer monitoring adopted at the USPTO is relatively less intrusive; other monitoring software may include more intrusive features, such as a facial recognition system, and in turn, lead to more negative consequences. Lastly, our study only suggests that an input-based control can be an effective complement to output-based controls. The USPTO already had output-based controls in place when introducing computer monitoring, and our results do not speak to whether an input control substitutes for output controls. Regardless, our research, to our knowledge, provides the first empirical evidence that computer monitoring affects telecommuters’ productivity and lays the groundwork for future research on the effects of computer monitoring in telecommuting arrangements.33
